content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 25, 2000


E. Clarke Moseley
Law Offices of Moseley & Leech
11001 East Valley Mall, Suite 200
El Monte, California 91731

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-99-312

Dear Mr. Moseley:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember George
Chapjian regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

I. QUESTION

     May Councilmember George Chapjian whose principal residence is within 300 feet of the
boundary of a proposed new residential development participate in decisions concerning the
proposal?

II. CONCLUSION

     If there will be no financial effect on Mr. Chapjian's property as explained below, he
would not have a conflict of interest.²




___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

² The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. This advice is applicable and
confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct and that all of the
material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.)

File No. A-99-312
Page 2

III. FACTS

Mr. George Chapjian is a newly elected councilmember for the City of Duarte. There is a
proposed new residential development project which is expected to come before the city council
in the near future. Mr. Chapjian's principal residence is within 300 feet of the proposed project.
Your letter states that in the opinion of Mr. Chapjian, at this time, there is no foreseeable
financial impact, either way, upon the value of his residence. In our telephone conversations of
December 29, 1999, and December 31, 1999, you indicated to me that the City of Duarte has a
population of less than 25,000 and that the proposed development project consists of new home
development of approximately 20 houses. You clarified that you were asking advice on behalf of
Mr. Chapjian and verified that his home is within 300 feet of the exterior boundary of the
proposed development project.

IV. ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest.

To say that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, is to conclude that it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step
analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given
governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b).) The following advice applies that eight-step
analysis.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) As a member of the Duarte City Council, Mr. Chapjian
is a "public official," for purposes of the Act (see Sections 82048, 82041), and the conflict of
interest rules apply to him.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only where the public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) The Commission has adopted a series of regulations
which define "making," "participating in making," and "influencing" a governmental decision,
and which provide certain exceptions. (Regulations 18702-18702.4.)

File No. A-99-312
Page 3

By deliberating and voting on the proposed development project, Councilmember
Chapjian would be making (see Regulation 18702.1) and participating in making (see Regulation
18702.2) governmental decisions. Thus, the Act's conflict of interest rules apply to this
situation.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. The "economic interests" from which conflicts of interest may arise are defined in
Regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, of these economic interests are held by a
public official is the third step in analyzing a potential conflict of interest under the Act. (See
Regulation 18700(b)(3).) There are five kinds of such economic interests:

•	A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment³ of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b));

•	A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $1,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);

•	A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, which aggregates to $250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);

•	A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $300 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4);

•	A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family—this is known as the
"personal financial effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5).

Here, Councilmember Chapjian has an economic interest in his personal residence.
(Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)⁴

_______________________________

³ An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent
child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the
official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103.)

⁴ Be advised that Mr. Chapjian may have additional economic interests that may result in conflicts of
interest which were not discernable from the information contained in your request.

File No. A-99-312
Page 4

To decide if Councilmember Chapjian has a conflict of interest arising from his
economic interest, the real property, it must first be determined if the real property is directly or
indirectly involved in the city council's decisions about the proposed development project. (See
Regulation 18700(b)(4).) Regulation 18704.2(a) prescribes specific rules for determining
whether a given real property economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision.
Applying these rules to Councilmember Chapjian's real property in the context of the proposed
development project, it appears that the real property is not directly involved in the upcoming
decisions about the proposal. Therefore, pursuant to Regulation 18704.2(b), the real property is
considered to be indirectly involved in the decisions.

Knowing the degree to which the real property is involved in the proposed development
project decisions, we can pick the appropriate standard for evaluating the "materiality"—that is,
the effect of the decisions on the real property. (See Regulation 18700(b)(5).) The "materiality standards" for indirectly involved real property economic interests are found in Regulation 18705.2(b).

Since Councilmember Chapjian's real property is within 300 feet of the boundaries of the
proposed development project, the rule in Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A) applies. Under that
subdivision, any financial effect—even a penny's worth—on the real property due to a city
council's decision about a proposed development project will be deemed to be material. Thus, if
the decision will have no financial effect whatsoever on his personal residence, then he does not
have a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18705(c)(2).)

To determine whether there will be any financial effect on a public official's real
property, in the past, we have suggested an approach that takes into consideration the factors
described in subdivision (b)(4) of Regulation 18705.2 (See, e.g., Hentschke Advice Letter,
No. A-97-058 and Fitzpatrick Advice Letter, No. A-93-054.) Subdivision (b)(4) provides as
follows:

"For a decision which is covered by subdivision (b)(1)(C) or (b)(2)(A) or (b)(3),
factors which shall be considered in determining whether the decision will have
the effects set forth in subdivision (b)(1) and (b)(2) include, but are not limited to:
(A) The proximity of the property which is the subject of the decision and the
magnitude of the proposed project or change in use in relationship to the property
in which the official has an interest;
(B) Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will affect the
development potential or income producing potential of the property;
(C) In addition to the foregoing, in the case of residential property, whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will result in a change to the character of
the neighborhood including, but not limited to, effect on traffic, view, privacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."

File No. A-99-312
Page 5

These factors may sensibly be adopted for a determination that there will be no
foreseeable financial effect on property governed by subdivision (b)(1)(A) as well. If after
reviewing the factors in Regulation 18705.2(b)(4), Mr. Chapjian determines that there is no
financial effect on his personal property, he may participate in the decision.5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division

LM:LD:tls

5 The Commission is currently considering issues of this type in the Phase 2 conflict of interest project. If
you would like to be included on our mailing list so you can be apprised of recent developments and interested
persons' meetings, please contact our office.

I:\Advice Ltrs\99312.wpd

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember whose home is located within 300 feet of a proposed residential development project can participate in city council decisions regarding that project under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The councilmember may participate in decisions regarding the proposed development if he determines there will be no foreseeable financial effect on his personal residence, applying the materiality factors set forth in the regulations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Clear FPPC advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. Document includes detailed legal analysis with specific regulatory citations. Some OCR quality issues noted in header but main text is clear and extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember George Chapjian whose principal residence is within 300 feet of the boundary of a proposed new residential development participate in decisions concerning the proposal?

CONCLUSION: If there will be no financial effect on Mr. Chapjian's property as explained below, he would not have a conflict of interest.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that Councilmember George Chapjian of Duarte can participate in city council decisions on a proposed residential development located within 300 feet of his home if there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his property. The letter applies the eight-step conflict of interest analysis to determine that while his residence is an economic interest, it is indirectly involved in the decision, and participation is permitted if no financial impact is foreseeable.
